questions.question: What is the purpose of an 'if' statement?
options: To execute block of code only if a condition is true
questions.answer: To execute block of code only if a condition is true

questions.question: What must wrap the condition in an if statement?
What happens if an 'if' condition is false?
Which keyword is used to provide an alternative block of code if the 'if' condition is false?
How do you check multiple specific conditions sequentially?
What evaluates to 'True' in C?
What evaluates to 'False' in C?
Can an 'else' block exist without an 'if' block preceding it?
options: Yes, it acts as a default
No, it must immediately follow an if or else if block
Yes, if compiled with special flags
questions.answer: No, it must immediately follow an if or else if block

questions.question: If `int x = 0; if (x) { printf("A"); } else { printf("B"); }`, what prints?
If there are no curly braces `{}` after an `if` statement, what happens?
options: Only the immediate next line is considered part of the 'if' block
questions.answer: Only the immediate next line is considered part of the 'if' block

questions.question: What does a switch statement compare a variable against?
Which keyword specifies a possible match in a switch block?
What is the purpose of 'break' inside a switch case?
options: It stops execution from falling through to the next case
It pauses the execution for user input
questions.answer: It stops execution from falling through to the next case

questions.question: What happens if you forget the 'break' statement?
options: Execution 'falls through', running the code in the next cases too
questions.answer: Execution 'falls through', running the code in the next cases too

questions.question: Which keyword is used to handle values that do not match any case?
Can you use standard strings (like "Hello") as a case condition in standard C?
options: No, cases must evaluate to integer constants (ints, chars, enums)
questions.answer: No, cases must evaluate to integer constants (ints, chars, enums)

questions.question: Is the 'default' case mandatory in every switch statement?
options: Only if there are more than 3 cases

questions.question: Where should the 'default' case be placed?
options: It must be the first item
It must be the last item
It can be placed anywhere, but it is conventional to put it last
questions.answer: It can be placed anywhere, but it is conventional to put it last

questions.question: If `x=2`, what happens in `switch(x){ case 1: print("A"); case 2: print("B"); case 3: print("C"); }` assuming no breaks?
Which data type is legally allowed as a switch controlling expression?
What does the 'goto' statement do?
options: Jumps unconditionally to a defined label in the same function
questions.answer: Jumps unconditionally to a defined label in the same function

questions.question: Why is 'goto' generally discouraged in modern programming?
options: It creates 'spaghetti code' that is hard to follow and maintain
questions.answer: It creates 'spaghetti code' that is hard to follow and maintain

questions.question: What is the one major scenario where 'goto' is still considered acceptable or idiomatic in C?
options: Centralized error handling and memory cleanup functions
questions.answer: Centralized error handling and memory cleanup functions

questions.question: How do you define a jump label for a goto statement?
Can 'goto' jump directly into a different function?
options: No, it can only jump within the scope of the current function
Yes, if it's in the same file
questions.answer: No, it can only jump within the scope of the current function

questions.question: What is 'switch fallthrough'?
options: A compiler error from a missing break
Intentionally omitting 'break' to allow multiple cases to share the same execution block
questions.answer: Intentionally omitting 'break' to allow multiple cases to share the same execution block

questions.question: Which of the following creates a fallthrough for testing if a char is a vowel 'a' or 'e'?
Which statement best describes standard control flow in C?
Because C has no `try/catch` exception blocks, how do standard C functions typically report errors to the calling control flow?
options: By returning a specific error code (like -1 or NULL)
questions.answer: By returning a specific error code (like -1 or NULL)

questions.question: Can an 'if' statement contain nested 'switch' statements, and vice versa?
options: Yes, C allows arbitrary nesting of valid control flow structures
questions.answer: Yes, C allows arbitrary nesting of valid control flow structures
